Task 6 完成。继续最后的 Task 7（薄渲染器）。

Task 6 done. Now Task 7: 薄渲染器 TombRaidMapRenderer.

This is the renderer. It needs to:
- Take a Phaser.Scene and a TombRaidMapManifest
- render(): floor tiles for rooms + corridors using floor.tile (192×192 single tile frame), walls (graphics), doors (graphics fill+stroke), chest placeholders (rectangles)
- getCollisionZones(): return all collision zones (room walls + corridor walls)
- isWalkable(x, y): false if inside any collision zone
- destroy(): cleanup created objects

For the test, mock phaser like map-renderer.test.ts. The test checks:
- render() creates floor images (one per tile) for rooms and corridors
- render() creates graphics ops for walls and doors
- chest placeholders rendered as rectangles (normal vs gilded colors)
- isWalkable returns false inside a room wall, true inside walkable
- getCollisionZones returns the merged list

Let me design the renderer:

```ts
import Phaser from 'phaser';
import {
  TOMB_RAID_WALL_THICKNESS,
  type TombRaidChestSpawn,
  type TombRaidMapManifest,
  type TombRaidRect,
} from './tombRaidMapState';

const FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_SIZE = 192;
const FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_X = FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_SIZE;
const FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_Y = 0;
const FLOOR_TILE_FRAME = 'single-floor-tile-192';
const FLOOR_DISPLAY_SIZE = 192; // use native tile size (no downscale)
const DOOR_FILL_COLOR = 0x5c4221;
const DOOR_STROKE_COLOR = 0xa37435;
const DOOR_STROKE_WIDTH = 2;
const WALL_COLOR = 0x1a171c;
const CHEST_NORMAL_COLOR = 0x6b4a1f;
const CHEST_GILDED_COLOR = 0xd4a017;

export class TombRaidMapRenderer {
  private objects: Phaser.GameObjects.GameObject[] = [];
  private collisionZones: TombRaidRect[] = [];

  constructor(private scene: Phaser.Scene, private manifest: TombRaidMapManifest) {}

  render(): void {
    this.destroy();
    this.collisionZones = [];
    // floors + walls for rooms
    for (const room of this.manifest.rooms) {
      this.renderFloor(room.bounds);
      this.renderWalls(room.collisionZones);
      this.collisionZones.push(...room.collisionZones);
    }
    // floors + walls for corridors
    for (const corridor of this.manifest.corridors) {
      this.renderFloor(corridor.bounds);
      this.renderWalls(corridor.collisionZones);
      this.collisionZones.push(...corridor.collisionZones);
    }
    // doors
    for (const door of this.manifest.doors) {
      this.renderDoor(door.bounds);
    }
    // chests
    for (const chest of this.manifest.chests) {
      this.renderChest(chest);
    }
  }

  private ensureFloorTileFrame(textureKey: string): string {
    const texture = this.scene.textures.get(textureKey);
    if (!texture.has(FLOOR_TILE_FRAME)) {
      texture.add(FLOOR_TILE_FRAME, 0, FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_X, FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_Y, FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_SIZE, FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_SIZE);
    }
    return FLOOR_TILE_FRAME;
  }

  private renderFloor(area: TombRaidRect): void {
    const tileKey = this.manifest.floorTile.assetKey;
    if (!this.scene.textures.exists(tileKey)) return;
    const frame = this.ensureFloorTileFrame(tileKey);
    const size = FLOOR_DISPLAY_SIZE;
    const columns = Math.ceil(area.width / size);
    const rows = Math.ceil(area.height / size);
    for (let row = 0; row < rows; row++) {
      for (let col = 0; col < columns; col++) {
        const tile = this.scene.add.image(
          area.x + col * size + size / 2,
          area.y + row * size + size / 2,
          tileKey,
          frame,
        );
        tile.setOrigin(0.5, 0.5);
        tile.setDisplaySize(size, size);
        tile.setDepth(0);
        this.objects.push(tile);
      }
    }
  }

  private renderWalls(zones: readonly TombRaidRect[]): void {
    const gfx = this.scene.add.graphics();
    gfx.fillStyle(WALL_COLOR, 1);
    for (const zone of zones) {
      gfx.fillRect(zone.x, zone.y, zone.width, zone.height);
    }
    gfx.setDepth(1);
    this.objects.push(gfx);
  }

  private renderDoor(bounds: TombRaidRect): void {
    const gfx = this.scene.add.graphics();
    gfx.fillStyle(DOOR_FILL_COLOR, 1);
    gfx.fillRect(bounds.x, bounds.y, bounds.width, bounds.height);
    gfx.lineStyle(DOOR_STROKE_WIDTH, DOOR_STROKE_COLOR, 1);
    gfx.strokeRect(bounds.x, bounds.y, bounds.width, bounds.height);
    gfx.setDepth(6);
    this.objects.push(gfx);
  }

  private renderChest(chest: TombRaidChestSpawn): void {
    const color = chest.kind === 'gilded' ? CHEST_GILDED_COLOR : CHEST_NORMAL_COLOR;
    const rect = this.scene.add.rectangle(
      chest.bounds.x + chest.bounds.width / 2,
      chest.bounds.y + chest.bounds.height / 2,
      chest.bounds.width,
      chest.bounds.height,
      color,
      1,
    );
    rect.setOrigin(0.5, 0.5);
    rect.setDepth(3);
    this.objects.push(rect);
  }

  getCollisionZones(): TombRaidRect[] {
    return this.collisionZones;
  }

  isWalkable(x: number, y: number): boolean {
    return this.collisionZones.every((zone) => !rectContains(zone, x, y));
  }

  destroy(): void {
    for (const obj of this.objects) {
      obj.destroy();
    }
    this.objects = [];
  }
}
```

Wait, I'm using `rectContains` but didn't import it. Let me import it. Also the collision zone rectangles are plain objects `{x,y,width,height}`, not Phaser.Geom.Rectangle. For `isWalkable`, I'll use the imported `rectContains` helper. Let me import it.

Hmm, but the existing `CollisionManager` uses `Phaser.Geom.Rectangle.contains`. For the tomb-raid renderer, since the manifest already has plain rects and the generator is pure TS, using the `rectContains` helper from tombRaidMapState keeps it Phaser-independent (good for testability). Test assertions:

- walls: graphics fillStyle/fillRect called for room collision zones

- isWalkable: a point inside a wall returns false, a point in open floor returns true

```ts
it('renders chest placeholders as rectangles at depth 3 with distinct colors', () => {
  const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 34, roomCount: 18 });
  const mock = createMockScene();
  const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);
  renderer.render();

  const chestRects = mock.rectangles.filter((r) => r.depth === 3);
  expect(chestRects.length).toBe(manifest.chests.length);
  // rectangles are pushed in manifest.chests order
  const GILDED = 0xd4a017;
  const NORMAL = 0x6b4a1f;
  chestRects.forEach((rect, i) => {
    const expected = manifest.chests[i]!.kind === 'gilded' ? GILDED : NORMAL;
    expect(rect.color).toBe(expected);
  });
  // at least one gilded (vault always has 1) → gilded color present
  expect(chestRects.some((r) => r.color === GILDED)).toBe(true);
});
```

```ts
const destroyCalls = [...].filter((obj) => (obj.destroy as ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>).mock).length;

Each mock object's `destroy` is `vi.fn()`. `vi.fn()` returns a mock function with `.mock` property. `(obj.destroy as ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>).mock` accesses the mock's calls. A vi.fn() always has a `.mock` object (truthy). So this filter counts all objects that have a `.mock` on their destroy — i.e., all mock objects. So `destroyCalls` = total mock objects = `before`. ✓. So `mock.images` accumulates across renders. The test expects `mock.images.length` to stay the same after re-render, but it would actually double.

So this test would FAIL as written. I need to fix either the test or the understanding.

The renderer's contract: `render()` calls `destroy()` (which destroys game objects in the real Phaser scene, removing them from display) then re-creates. In real Phaser, `obj.destroy()` removes the object from the scene's display list. But in the mock, `destroy` is a no-op vi.fn, so the mock arrays don't reflect removal.

To test "no accumulation" correctly, the mock's `destroy` should remove the object from the tracking arrays, OR the test should track something else.

Cleaner approach: track a `liveCount` that increments on create and decrements on destroy. Let me restructure the mock:

```ts
function createMockScene() {
  const images: ...[] = [];
  ...
  let liveCount = 0;
  return {
    images, rectangles, graphicsOps, graphicsObjects, textureAdd,
    liveCount: () => liveCount,
    scene: {
      ...
      add: {
        image: vi.fn((...) => {
          liveCount++;
          const image = chainableObject({...});
          image.destroy = () => { liveCount--; };
          images.push(image);
          return image;
        }),
        ...
      }
    }
  };
}
```

So `liveCount` after second render === liveCount after first render. ✓

Edit A — replace `chainableObject` and `createMockScene`:

old:
```ts
function chainableObject(extra: Record<string, unknown> = {}): Record<string, unknown> {
  const object: Record<string, unknown> = { ...extra };
  object.setOrigin = () => object;
  object.setDepth = (depth: number) => { object.depth = depth; return object; };
  object.setDisplaySize = (w: number, h: number) => { object.displayWidth = w; object.displayHeight = h; return object; };
  object.setStrokeStyle = () => object;
  object.setInteractive = () => object;
  object.destroy = vi.fn();
  return object;
}

function createMockScene() {
  const images: Record<string, unknown>[] = [];
  const rectangles: Record<string, unknown>[] = [];
  const graphicsOps: Record<string, unknown>[] = [];
  const graphicsObjects: Record<string, unknown>[] = [];
  const textureFrames = new Set<string>();
  const textureAdd = vi.fn((name: string) => { textureFrames.add(name); return { name }; });

  return {
    images,
    rectangles,
    graphicsOps,
    graphicsObjects,
    textureAdd,
    scene: {
      textures: {
        exists: (key: string) => key === 'floor.tile',
        get: (_key: string) => ({
          has: (frame: string) => textureFrames.has(frame),
          add: textureAdd,
        }),
      },
      add: {
        image: vi.fn((x: number, y: number, key: string, frame?: string) => {
          const image = chainableObject({ x, y, key, frame });
          images.push(image);
          return image;
        }),
        rectangle: vi.fn((x: number, y: number, width: number, height: number, color?: number, alpha?: number) => {
          const rectangle = chainableObject({ x, y, width, height, color, alpha });
          rectangles.push(rectangle);
          return rectangle;
        }),
        graphics: vi.fn(() => {
          const graphics = chainableObject();
          graphics.fillStyle = (color: number, alpha = 1) => { graphicsOps.push({ op: 'fillStyle', color, alpha }); return graphics; };
          graphics.lineStyle = (width: number, color: number, alpha = 1) => { graphicsOps.push({ op: 'lineStyle', width, color, alpha }); return graphics; };
          graphics.fillRect = (x: number, y: number, width: number, height: number) => { graphicsOps.push({ op: 'fillRect', x, y, width, height }); return graphics; };
          graphics.strokeRect = (x: number, y: number, width: number, height: number) => { graphicsOps.push({ op: 'strokeRect', x, y, width, height }); return graphics; };
          graphics.clear = () => graphics;
          graphicsObjects.push(graphics);
          return graphics;
        }),
      },
    },
  };
}
```

new: add liveCount tracking.

```ts
function chainableObject(extra: Record<string, unknown>, onDestroy: () => void): Record<string, unknown> {
  const object: Record<string, unknown> = { ...extra };
  object.setOrigin = () => object;
  object.setDepth = (depth: number) => { object.depth = depth; return object; };
  object.setDisplaySize = (w: number, h: number) => { object.displayWidth = w; object.displayHeight = h; return object; };
  object.setStrokeStyle = () => object;
  object.setInteractive = () => object;
  object.destroy = () => { onDestroy(); };
  return object;
}

function createMockScene() {
  const images: Record<string, unknown>[] = [];
  const rectangles: Record<string, unknown>[] = [];
  const graphicsOps: Record<string, unknown>[] = [];
  const graphicsObjects: Record<string, unknown>[] = [];
  const textureFrames = new Set<string>();
  const textureAdd = vi.fn((name: string) => { textureFrames.add(name); return { name }; });
  let liveCount = 0;
  const born = () => { liveCount += 1; };
  const died = () => { liveCount -= 1; };

  return {
    images,
    rectangles,
    graphicsOps,
    graphicsObjects,
    textureAdd,
    liveCount: () => liveCount,
    scene: {
      textures: {
        exists: (key: string) => key === 'floor.tile',
        get: (_key: string) => ({
          has: (frame: string) => textureFrames.has(frame),
          add: textureAdd,
        }),
      },
      add: {
        image: vi.fn((x: number, y: number, key: string, frame?: string) => {
          const image = chainableObject({ x, y, key, frame }, died);
          born();
          images.push(image);
          return image;
        }),
        rectangle: vi.fn((x: number, y: number, width: number, height: number, color?: number, alpha?: number) => {
          const rectangle = chainableObject({ x, y, width, height, color, alpha }, died);
          born();
          rectangles.push(rectangle);
          return rectangle;
        }),
        graphics: vi.fn(() => {
          const graphics = chainableObject({}, died);
          born();
          graphics.fillStyle = (color: number, alpha = 1) => { graphicsOps.push({ op: 'fillStyle', color, alpha }); return graphics; };
          graphics.lineStyle = (width: number, color: number, alpha = 1) => { graphicsOps.push({ op: 'lineStyle', width, color, alpha }); return graphics; };
          graphics.fillRect = (x: number, y: number, width: number, height: number) => { graphicsOps.push({ op: 'fillRect', x, y, width, height }); return graphics; };
          graphics.strokeRect = (x: number, y: number, width: number, height: number) => { graphicsOps.push({ op: 'strokeRect', x, y, width, height }); return graphics; };
          graphics.clear = () => graphics;
          graphicsObjects.push(graphics);
          return graphics;
        }),
      },
    },
  };
}
```

Then fix the destroy test:
```ts
it('destroy clears rendered objects and is idempotent', () => {
  const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 36, roomCount: 16 });
  const mock = createMockScene();
  const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);

  renderer.render();
  expect(mock.liveCount()).toBeGreaterThan(0);

  renderer.destroy();
  expect(mock.liveCount()).toBe(0);
  expect(renderer.getCollisionZones()).toEqual([]);
  expect(() => renderer.destroy()).not.toThrow();
  expect(mock.liveCount()).toBe(0);
});
```

And re-render test:
```ts
it('re-render replaces previous objects (no accumulation across two renders)', () => {
  const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 37, roomCount: 16 });
  const mock = createMockScene();
  const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);

  renderer.render();
  const firstLiveCount = mock.liveCount();
  expect(firstLiveCount).toBeGreaterThan(0);
  renderer.render();
  expect(mock.liveCount()).toBe(firstLiveCount);
});
```

And chest test (color-based):
```ts
it('renders chest placeholders as rectangles at depth 3 with distinct colors', () => {
  const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 34, roomCount: 18 });
  const mock = createMockScene();
  const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);

  renderer.render();

  const chestRects = mock.rectangles.filter((r) => r.depth === 3);
  expect(chestRects.length).toBe(manifest.chests.length);
  const GILDED = 0xd4a017;
  const NORMAL = 0x6b4a1f;
  // rectangles are pushed in manifest.chests order
  chestRects.forEach((rect, i) => {
    const expected = manifest.chests[i]!.kind === 'gilded' ? GILDED : NORMAL;
    expect(rect.color).toBe(expected);
  });
  // vault always contributes 1 gilded → gilded color present, and differs from normal
  expect(chestRects.some((r) => r.color === GILDED)).toBe(true);
  expect(GILDED).not.toBe(NORMAL);
});
```

Good. Now let me also reconsider: the `images` array still accumulates across renders (old image objects stay in `images` even after destroy). The floor-tile test only renders once, so that's fine. The "renders floor tiles" test checks `mock.images.filter(...).length` > 0 and per-image properties — fine after single render.

But the "draws each door" test: `mock.graphicsObjects.filter((g) => g.depth === 6)` — single render, fine.

OK. Now, there's another subtle issue with the floor tile test: `expect(mock.textureAdd).toHaveBeenCalledWith('single-floor-tile-192', 0, 192, 0, 192, 192)`. The renderer calls `texture.add(FLOOR_TILE_FRAME, 0, FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_X, FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_Y, FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_SIZE, FLOOR_SOURCE_TILE_SIZE)` = `texture.add('single-floor-tile-192', 0, 192, 0, 192, 192)`. The mock's `textureAdd = vi.fn((name) => {...})` — it's called with all 6 args. `toHaveBeenCalledWith('single-floor-tile-192', 0, 192, 0, 192, 192)` ✓.

But wait: `ensureFloorTileFrame` only calls `texture.add` if `!texture.has(frame)`. The mock's `has` checks `textureFrames.has(frame)`. Initially empty, so first call adds. But the renderer calls `ensureFloorTileFrame` once per `renderFloor` call (per room + per corridor). The first call adds the frame (textureAdd called, textureFrames now has it). Subsequent calls: `texture.has(frame)` is true → no add. So `textureAdd` is called exactly once. `toHaveBeenCalledWith` passes. ✓.

Good. Now let me also verify the "draws room + corridor walls" test. `mock.graphicsObjects.filter((g) => g.depth === 1)` — walls are at depth 1. The renderer calls `renderWalls` once per room and once per corridor, each creating a graphics at depth 1. So `wallGraphics.length` = rooms.length + corridors.length > 0. ✓. And `fillRects` ops > 0. ✓.

Now the "draws each door" test: `doorGraphics = graphicsObjects.filter(g => g.depth === 6)`. Each door creates one graphics at depth 6. So `doorGraphics.length === manifest.doors.length`. ✓.

Wait, but graphicsObjects accumulates ALL graphics (walls at depth 1, doors at depth 6). The filter by depth 6 isolates doors. ✓. Single render, so no accumulation issue.

Now, one more: the chest test filters `mock.rectangles.filter((r) => r.depth === 3)`. Rectangles are only created for chests (at depth 3). So all rectangles are chests. `chestRects.length === manifest.chests.length`. ✓. And rectangles pushed in chests order. ✓.

Good. Now let me also reconsider the `isWalkable` test. `firstRoom.bounds.x, firstRoom.bounds.y` — is that inside a wall zone? The first room's collisionZones include `{x: bounds.x, y: bounds.y, width: bounds.width, height: 12}` (top wall). The point (bounds.x, bounds.y) is at the corner — `rectContains` uses `x >= rect.x && x <= rect.x+width && y >= rect.y && y <= rect.y+height`. (bounds.x, bounds.y) is in the top wall zone (x=bounds.x is >= bounds.x and <= bounds.x+width; y=bounds.y is >= bounds.y and <= bounds.y+12). ✓. So `zones.some(rectContains)` true, `isWalkable` false. ✓.

For Task 7 chest test, the exact old block:
```ts
  it('renders chest placeholders as rectangles at depth 3 with distinct colors', () => {
    const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 34, roomCount: 18 });
    const mock = createMockScene();
    const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);

    renderer.render();

    const chestRects = mock.rectangles.filter((r) => r.depth === 3);
    expect(chestRects.length).toBe(manifest.chests.length);
    const gildedColors = new Set(chestRects.filter((r) => r.kind === 'gilded').map((r) => r.color));
    const normalColors = new Set(chestRects.filter((r) => r.kind === 'normal').map((r) => r.color));
    // gilded color must differ from normal color
    expect([...gildedColors][0]).not.toBe([...normalColors][0]);
  });
```

For Task 7 destroy test:
```ts
  it('destroy clears rendered objects and is idempotent', () => {
    const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 36, roomCount: 16 });
    const mock = createMockScene();
    const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);

    renderer.render();
    const before = mock.images.length + mock.graphicsObjects.length + mock.rectangles.length;
    expect(before).toBeGreaterThan(0);

    renderer.destroy();
    const destroyCalls = [
      ...mock.images,
      ...mock.graphicsObjects,
      ...mock.rectangles,
    ].filter((obj) => (obj.destroy as ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>).mock).length;
    expect(destroyCalls).toBe(before);
    expect(renderer.getCollisionZones()).toEqual([]);
    expect(() => renderer.destroy()).not.toThrow();
  });
```

For Task 7 re-render test:
```ts
  it('re-render replaces previous objects (no accumulation across two renders)', () => {
    const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 37, roomCount: 16 });
    const mock = createMockScene();
    const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);

    renderer.render();
    const firstImageCount = mock.images.length;
    renderer.render();
    expect(mock.images.length).toBe(firstImageCount);
  });
```

Now let me write the new versions.

Chest test new:
```ts
  it('renders chest placeholders as rectangles at depth 3 with distinct colors', () => {
    const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 34, roomCount: 18 });
    const mock = createMockScene();
    const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);

    renderer.render();

    const chestRects = mock.rectangles.filter((r) => r.depth === 3);
    expect(chestRects.length).toBe(manifest.chests.length);
    const GILDED = 0xd4a017;
    const NORMAL = 0x6b4a1f;
    // rectangles are pushed in manifest.chests order, so indices line up
    chestRects.forEach((rect, i) => {
      const expected = manifest.chests[i]!.kind === 'gilded' ? GILDED : NORMAL;
      expect(rect.color).toBe(expected);
    });
    // vault always contributes 1 gilded chest → gilded color present and differs from normal
    expect(chestRects.some((r) => r.color === GILDED)).toBe(true);
    expect(GILDED).not.toBe(NORMAL);
  });
```

Destroy test new:
```ts
  it('destroy clears rendered objects and is idempotent', () => {
    const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 36, roomCount: 16 });
    const mock = createMockScene();
    const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);

    renderer.render();
    const tracked = [...mock.images, ...mock.graphicsObjects, ...mock.rectangles];
    expect(tracked.length).toBeGreaterThan(0);
    expect(renderer.getCollisionZones().length).toBeGreaterThan(0);

    renderer.destroy();
    expect(renderer.getCollisionZones()).toEqual([]);
    expect(
      tracked.every((obj) => (obj.destroy as ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>).mock.calls.length >= 1),
    ).toBe(true);
    expect(() => renderer.destroy()).not.toThrow();
  });
```

Re-render test new:
```ts
  it('re-render replaces previous objects (no accumulation across two renders)', () => {
    const manifest = generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 37, roomCount: 16 });
    const mock = createMockScene();
    const renderer = new TombRaidMapRenderer(mock.scene as never, manifest);

    renderer.render();
    const zoneCount = renderer.getCollisionZones().length;
    expect(zoneCount).toBeGreaterThan(0);

    renderer.render();
    // renderer state is rebuilt from the manifest, not accumulated
    expect(renderer.getCollisionZones().length).toBe(zoneCount);
  });
```

Good. Now let me also reconsider: in the destroy test, `(obj.destroy as ReturnType<typeof vi.fn>).mock.calls.length` — `obj.destroy` is `vi.fn()` (from chainableObject). Its `.mock.calls` is an array. After `renderer.destroy()` calls `obj.destroy()`, calls.length >= 1. ✓.

But wait — `chainableObject` sets `object.destroy = vi.fn();`. With `restoreMocks: true` and `clearMocks: true` in vitest config, mocks are cleared between tests. Within a test, the vi.fn accumulates calls. `renderer.render()` does NOT call destroy on the freshly created objects (it only calls `this.destroy()` at the START of render, when `this.objects` is empty — so no destroy calls during first render). Then `renderer.destroy()` calls destroy on all objects → calls.length = 1 each. ✓. Good.

Wait, actually: `render()` starts with `this.destroy()`. On first render, `this.objects` is `[]` (initial), so `this.destroy()` loops over nothing. Then creates objects. So first render: no destroy calls on the new objects. ✓. Then explicit `renderer.destroy()`: calls destroy on each → calls.length = 1. ✓.

Now let me also verify the `selectConnectedCells` reproducibility concern in Task 6's full-pipeline reproducibility test. The pipeline calls selectConnectedCells, generateRoomRectangles, assignRoomKinds, buildSpanningTree, addRingEdges, buildCorridorsAndDoors, distributeChests — ALL using the SAME `rng` instance (created once). So the sequence of rng calls is deterministic for a given seed. `generateTombRaidMap(params)` called twice with same params → same rng sequence → same manifest. ✓. The test `expect(generateTombRaidMap(params)).toEqual(generateTombRaidMap(params))` passes. ✓.

Now, one more: Task 6 test "clamps out-of-range room counts" — `generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 1, roomCount: 5 }).roomCount` should be 16. With roomCount=5, clamp(5,16,20)=16. selectConnectedCells(rng, 16, 0) → 16 cells. rooms.length=16. manifest.roomCount=16. ✓. And `generateTombRaidMap({ seed: 1, roomCount: 99 }).roomCount` = 20. clamp(99,16,20)=20. But selectConnectedCells(rng, 20, 0) — can it reach 20 cells? Grid is 5×4=20 cells total. Starting at 0, randomized BFS growth should fill all 20 (grid is connected). But is it GUARANTEED to reach 20? The growth picks candidates with unvisited neighbors and adds one. It will keep growing until visited.size == 20 (all cells). Since the grid is fully connected, candidates is non-empty until all 20 are visited. So yes, reaches 20. ✓. 

But there's a subtle risk: `selectConnectedCells` with roomCount=20 — the while loop runs until visited.size < 20. Each iteration adds exactly one cell (when candidates non-empty and the picked candidate has unvisited neighbor). Starting from 0, it can always grow to 20. ✓.

Hmm, but actually there's a potential issue: could the randomized BFS growth get "stuck" where candidates is non-empty but all picked candidates... no. The loop: pick a random candidate (which by filter has >=1 unvisited neighbor), shuffle its neighbors, take first unvisited. Since candidate has >=1 unvisited neighbor, `unvisited` is non-empty, so `unvisited[0]` exists, add it. So each iteration grows by 1. Reaches 20. ✓.

Good. Now let me also check: `selectConnectedCells` test in Task 2 uses `selectConnectedCells(rng, 16, 0)` and expects 16 cells. But note the test does `const rng = createRng(2026); const cells = selectConnectedCells(rng, 16, 0);` — passes the SAME rng. Fine. ✓.

But the reproducibility test: `selectConnectedCells(createRng(5), 18, 0)` twice — each creates a FRESH rng with seed 5. Same seed → same sequence → same result. ✓.

OK, I'm fairly confident now. Let me also check one thing in Task 4 test: `buildSpanningTree(createRng(111), cells)` where `cells = selectConnectedCells(createRng(111), 16, 0)`. Note: `selectConnectedCells` consumes rng, then `buildSpanningTree` gets a FRESH `createRng(111)`. So they're independent fresh rngs. Fine for the test. ✓. The test doesn't require them to share rng.

Now, the `buildSpanningTree` test: `tree` has `cells.length - 1` edges. With 16 cells → 15 edges. ✓. Union-find connectivity: all cells in one component. ✓.

`addRingEdges` test with probability 1: adds every non-tree edge between selected 4-neighbors. The test checks ring edges are non-tree and between neighbors, no dupes. ✓.

`buildCorridorsAndDoors` test: 1 corridor + 2 doors per edge. corridors.length = tree.length. doors.length = tree.length * 2. ✓.

Now, the corridor orientation test: for horizontal edge (same row), `corridor.bounds.height === TOMB_RAID_CORRIDOR_THICKNESS` (192). ✓ (buildCorridor horizontal sets height=192). And `corridor.bounds.x + corridor.bounds.width > aRect.x + aRect.width - 1` — the corridor spans from fromRect.right to toRect.left, so corridor.right = toRect.x > fromRect.right. But the test uses `aRect` (which might be fromCell or toCell depending on canonical order). Hmm. Let me check: the test does `const [aCell, bCell] = tree[i]!; const aRect = rectByCell.get(aCell)!;`. Then for horizontal: `expect(corridor.bounds.x + corridor.bounds.width).toBeGreaterThan(aRect.x + aRect.width - 1)`. 

The corridor goes from fromRect.right to toRect.left. corridor.bounds.x + corridor.bounds.width = toRect.x (the right end is at toRect's left edge). Is toRect.x > aRect.x + aRect.width - 1? 

If aCell is the left room (fromCell), then aRect = fromRect, aRect.x + aRect.width = fromRect.right. toRect.x (corridor right end) > fromRect.right - 1 → yes (toRect is to the right of fromRect, with corridor between). ✓.

If aCell is the right room (toCell), then aRect = toRect. aRect.x + aRect.width = toRect.right. corridor right end = toRect.x = aRect.x. Is toRect.x > toRect.right - 1? toRect.x > toRect.x + toRect.width - 1 → 0 > toRect.width - 1 → toRect.width < 1. FALSE (room width >= 640). So the assertion would FAIL when aCell is the right room!

This is a bug in the Task 4 test. The assertion assumes aCell is the left room, but `tree[i]` edge order is [current, next] from the DFS, which may be either direction.

I need to fix this test assertion. Let me make it direction-agnostic: check that the corridor spans BETWEEN the two rooms. For horizontal: corridor.x is between min(aRect.right, bRect.right) and max(aRect.left, bRect.left)... actually simpler: corridor.x >= min(aRect.x, bRect.x) and corridor.right <= max(aRect.right, bRect.right), and corridor connects them.

Actually, the cleanest assertion: the corridor's x-range overlaps the gap between the two rooms. For horizontal edge, the corridor.x should be >= the left room's right edge, and corridor.right <= the right room's left edge. Let me compute left/right rooms:

```ts
if (aRow === bRow) {
  expect(corridor.orientation).toBe('horizontal');
  expect(corridor.bounds.height).toBe(TOMB_RAID_CORRIDOR_THICKNESS);
  const leftRect = aRect.x <= bRect.x ? aRect : bRect;
  const rightRect = aRect.x <= bRect.x ? bRect : aRect;
  expect(corridor.bounds.x).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(leftRect.x + leftRect.width);
  expect(corridor.bounds.x + corridor.bounds.width).toBeLessThanOrEqual(rightRect.x);
}
```

Wait, but corridor.x = fromRect.right (exactly), and corridor.right = toRect.x (exactly). So corridor.x = leftRect.right, corridor.right = rightRect.x. So:
- `corridor.bounds.x >= leftRect.x + leftRect.width` → equal. ✓.
- `corridor.bounds.x + corridor.bounds.width <= rightRect.x` → equal. ✓.

For vertical similarly:
```ts
} else {
  expect(aCol).toBe(bCol);
  expect(corridor.orientation).toBe('vertical');
  expect(corridor.bounds.width).toBe(TOMB_RAID_CORRIDOR_THICKNESS);
  const topRect = aRect.y <= bRect.y ? aRect : bRect;

  expect(corridor.bounds.y + corridor.bounds.height).toBeLessThanOrEqual(bottomRect.y);

```

This is direction-agnostic and correct. Let me fix the Task 4 test this way.

Also, `corridor.fromRoomId` / `toRoomId` — the test asserts `fromRoomId === room-${aCell}` and `toRoomId === room-${bCell}`. But `buildCorridor` canonicalizes order (left→right / top→bottom), so `fromRoomId` might be `room-${bCell}` if bCell is the left/top one! This is another direction bug.

Wait, let me re-check buildCorridor. It sets:
```ts
let fromCell = aCell; let toCell = bCell;
if (orientation === 'horizontal' && cellColRow(bCell).col < cellColRow(aCell).col) {
  fromCell = bCell; toCell = aCell; ...
}
```
So fromRoomId = `room-${fromCell}` which is the LEFT cell (smaller col). If tree[i] = [aCell, bCell] where aCell is the right cell, then fromCell = bCell, so fromRoomId = `room-${bCell}`, NOT `room-${aCell}`.

So the test assertion `expect(corridor.fromRoomId).toBe(\`room-${aCell}\`)` would FAIL when aCell is the right cell.

I need to fix this too. The test should check that fromRoomId/toRoomId are the two room ids in canonical order (left/right or top/bottom), not necessarily matching [aCell, bCell].

Let me fix: assert `{fromRoomId, toRoomId}` as a set equals `{room-${aCell}, room-${bCell}}`, and fromRoomId is the left/top one.

```ts
const expectedRooms = new Set([`room-${aCell}`, `room-${bCell}`]);
expect(expectedRooms.has(corridor.fromRoomId)).toBe(true);
expect(expectedRooms.has(corridor.toRoomId)).toBe(true);
expect(corridor.fromRoomId).not.toBe(corridor.toRoomId);
```

That's direction-agnostic. Good.

So the Task 4 test fix replaces the whole `corridors.forEach(...)` block. Let me write the corrected version:

```ts
    corridors.forEach((corridor, i) => {
      const [aCell, bCell] = tree[i]!;
      const aRect = rectByCell.get(aCell)!;
      const bRect = rectByCell.get(bCell)!;

      const expectedRooms = new Set([`room-${aCell}`, `room-${bCell}`]);
      expect(expectedRooms.has(corridor.fromRoomId)).toBe(true);
      expect(expectedRooms.has(corridor.toRoomId)).toBe(true);
      expect(corridor.fromRoomId).not.toBe(corridor.toRoomId);

      const { col: aCol, row: aRow } = cellColRow(aCell);
      const { col: bCol, row: bRow } = cellColRow(bCell);
      if (aRow === bRow) {
        expect(corridor.orientation).toBe('horizontal');
        expect(corridor.bounds.height).toBe(TOMB_RAID_CORRIDOR_THICKNESS);
        const leftRect = aRect.x <= bRect.x ? aRect : bRect;
        const rightRect = aRect.x <= bRect.x ? bRect : aRect;
        expect(corridor.bounds.x).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(leftRect.x + leftRect.width);
        expect(corridor.bounds.x + corridor.bounds.width).toBeLessThanOrEqual(rightRect.x);

        expect(aCol).toBe(bCol);
        expect(corridor.orientation).toBe('vertical');
        expect(corridor.bounds.width).toBe(TOMB_RAID_CORRIDOR_THICKNESS);
        const topRect = aRect.y <= bRect.y ? aRect : bRect;

        expect(corridor.bounds.y).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(topRect.y + topRect.height);
        expect(corridor.bounds.y + corridor.bounds.height).toBeLessThanOrEqual(bottomRect.y);
      }
    });
```

Good. Now I have the full set of fixes. Let me also double-check: are `aCol`/`bCol` used? In the vertical branch, `expect(aCol).toBe(bCol)`. Yes used. In horizontal branch, aCol/bCol not used but they're destructured — `noUnusedLocals` would flag if unused. But wait, in the horizontal branch we don't use aCol/bCol, but they ARE used in the vertical branch. Since both branches are in the same forEach, aCol/bCol are declared at the top of the loop body and used in at least one branch. TypeScript's noUnusedLocals checks if a variable is EVER used, not per-branch. aCol is used in `expect(aCol).toBe(bCol)` (vertical branch). So aCol/bCol are "used". ✓. Similarly aRow/bRow used in `if (aRow === bRow)`. ✓. No unused warning.

Actually, this is a very large plan. Let me think about how the enemies work. Looking at PlayScene, the game uses Phaser. The enemies need:
- A position
- HP
- An update method that takes delta time and the player position (and possibly the map for collision/walkable checks)
- An attack pattern with cooldowns
- Contact damage
- Movement
- A way to spawn projectiles/effects
- A way to take damage
- A death state

The tricky part: the spec mentions things like "电话爆炸留振铃区 2s 内再伤 10", "粉笔尘云 物理攻击半伤", "AoE/范围攻击 1.5 倍伤". So the damage system needs to know about damage categories (physical vs AoE) so the chalk dust cloud can apply damage reduction/multipliers.

Also for the summon core danYuxuanBody, there are 4 mechanisms (A/B/C/D) that need careful state tracking:
- A: every 30s summon a blood eye at random empty spot (200px from player); if alive blood eyes >= 3, cooldown but no summon
- B: body death → all bound heads die
- C: while body alive, dead heads revive after 20s at their death location
- D: 30% chance per head kill to permanently mark body position on minimap

And: max 2 bodies in the whole map.

For yangYunRed:
- HP 320, contact 22, speed 95
- Charge attack: interval 3s, windup 1.0s (red pillar), duration 0.7s, speed 320, dmg 50 + knockback
- Mechanism A shadow clone (HP<70% once): 2 blue yangYun phantoms (HP40, contact 8, speed 80, no charge), live 12s then dissipate, semi-transparent, untargetable
- Mechanism B floor crack wave (HP<70% every 8s): toward player, width 60, wave speed 200, dmg 28, slow 50% 1.5s, windup 0.6s red line warning
- Mechanism C phase 2 rage (HP<40%): attack interval 1.8s, charge speed 380, contact dmg + burn 3/s 3s, shadow clone CD halved, floor crack CD halved
- Kill drop: 100% vault key (but drops are plan 5... so this plan just emits an event/flag)
- Kill trigger: full-screen "理智正在消散" overlay + refresh +100% (this is an event the scene/HUD will handle; this plan emits an event/callback)

Since drops are plan 5 and HUD is plan 6, this plan should emit events/callbacks for things like:
- enemy death (with enemy kind, position) — for plan 5 drops
- yangYunRed death — special event for the overlay + refresh
- body position marked — for minimap (plan 6)
- debuff applied to player — for HUD (plan 6)
- player HP changed — for HUD (plan 6)
- player death — for scene

Let me design a clean interface. I'll use a callback-based approach (consistent with the project's style — EventEngine uses callbacks).

Let me think about the data flow:
- `CombatManager` owns: the PlayerCombat instance, the list of enemies, projectiles, zone effects, and a time accumulator.
- Each frame: `combatManager.update(deltaMs, playerPosition)` — updates enemies, projectiles, resolves collisions with player, applies debuffs, ticks debuffs, ticks cooldowns.
- Player attack: `combatManager.playerAttack(direction)` — uses current weapon (placeholder weak punch 5 dmg, melee range in front of player).
- Enemy AI: each enemy has `update(deltaMs, ctx)` where ctx provides player position, current time, spawn helpers (spawnProjectile, spawnZoneEffect), and walkable check.

For testability, I should separate the pure logic (positions, HP, cooldowns, damage math) from Phaser rendering. The enemies can have a `position` and a separate sprite/graphics handle. But to keep it manageable and testable in jsdom, I'll make the combat logic Phaser-light: enemies carry their state and a reference to an optional `view` (sprite/graphics) that they update. Tests can construct enemies without a view and assert on state.

Actually, looking at the input-manager test style, they mock Phaser heavily. For combat, I think the cleaner approach is:
- DamageType.ts: pure, no Phaser.
- PlayerCombat.ts: pure state (HP, debuffs, position, weapon id), no Phaser. Methods: takeDamage, applyDebuff, tickDebuffs, heal, isDead, getEffectiveSpeed.
- CombatManager.ts: pure logic operating on Enemy/PlayerCombat instances + projectile/zone lists. Uses rectContains/distance math (no Phaser.Geom) for jsdom testability. Has callbacks for events.
- Enemy.ts: base class with state + update logic. Subclasses implement AI. The base holds position, hp, speed, contactDamage, kind, dead flag. Rendering (sprite/graphics creation) is done via an injected `EnemyViewFactory`/`EnemyRenderer` so tests don't need Phaser. - `Projectile { id, position, velocity, damage, damageType, homingTarget?, lifetime, owner: 'enemy', textureKey | proceduralShape }`

CombatManager.update ticks these, moves projectiles (with optional homing), checks collisions vs player, applies damage/debuffs, removes expired.

Hmm, but burn is both a damage-over-time debuff and the spec lists "灼烧（持续伤害）" as a damage type. Let me unify: 
- `DamageType` enum: `Physical`, `Burn`, `Slow`, `Stun`, `Fear`, `Root`. 
- `Burn`/`Slow`/`Stun`/`Fear`/`Root` are debuffs with durations and effects.
- `Physical` is instant damage.
- For chalk dust multiplier, I need to know if the source is "physical melee" vs "AoE/area". So add a `DamageCategory = 'melee' | 'aoe'` field on the damage event, separate from DamageType. Chalk dust: melee category → 0.5x, aoe category → 1.5x. Burn (DoT) ticks are neither — they apply flat.

Let me define the damage model clearly in DamageType.ts:
```ts
export type DamageType = 'physical' | 'burn' | 'slow' | 'stun' | 'fear' | 'root';
export type DamageCategory = 'melee' | 'aoe' | 'dot';
export interface DebuffDef { type: ...; durationMs; ... }
export interface DamageInstance { amount: number; type: DamageType; category: DamageCategory; }
```

Debuffs: 
- burn: 2/s for 2s (eye laser), 3/s for 3s (yangYunRed phase 2 contact). So burn has dps + duration.
- slow: 60% for 2s (qin scream), 50% for 1.5s (floor crack), 30% (chalk dust vision — that's vision not speed; but spec says chalk "降低30%视野" not speed). Wait, chalk dust: "范围内降低 30% 视野" — that's a vision debuff, not movement. And "持续接触伤害 5/s". And "物理攻击半伤（云），AoE/范围攻击 1.5 倍伤". So chalk dust doesn't slow the player; it reduces vision (a HUD/camera concern, plan 6) and deals 5/s contact damage. So vision reduction is an event for plan 6.
- stun: cannot move/attack. (镇魂铃 applies 5s stun + 30%易伤 — but that's a gold consumable, plan 5.万魂幡 20% fear 2s — weapon plan 4.) For plan 3, stun/fear/root come from... actually looking at spec section 3.4, the debuffs are listed but the SOURCES in plan 3: blood hand grab → root 1s; floor crack → slow 50% 1.5s; yangYunRed phase 2 contact → burn 3/s 3s; eye laser → burn 2/s 2s; qin scream → slow 60% 2s. Fear/stun sources are weapons/consumables (plan 4/5). So plan 3 implements the debuff STATE MACHINE that can hold any debuff, but only applies slow/root/burn from its own enemies. Stun/fear states exist and are tickable but plan 3's enemies don't apply them (weapons/consumables will, plan 4/5). That's fine — the state machine is general.

Debuff definitions per spec:
- burn: { dps, durationMs } — variable per source. So burn is parameterized.
- slow: { multiplier (0.4 for 60% slow means speed*0.4; 0.5 for 50%), durationMs }
- stun: { durationMs } — no move/attack
- fear: { durationMs } — turn and flee (forced movement away from source)
- root: { durationMs } — can attack, can't move

The player's effective speed: base 200, multiplied by slow multiplier if slow active, 0 if stun/root/fear-flee... actually fear makes you flee (forced movement). For plan 3, I'll model: if stun or root active → speed 0 (can't move). If slow active → speed * slowMultiplier. If fear active → forced flee movement (speed away from fear source) — but implementing forced flee movement is the scene's job; PlayerCombat just reports `isFeared` and `fleeDirection`. Plan 3 provides the state; the scene reads it. Since plan 3 doesn't build the scene, I'll expose the state and a `getMovementOverride()` that returns either {type:'locked'} or {type:'flee', direction} or {type:'normal', speedMultiplier}.

Now, the enemy AI update signature. Each enemy:
```ts
abstract class Enemy {
  abstract readonly kind: EnemyKind;
  position: {x,y};
  hp: number;
  maxHp: number;
  speed: number;
  contactDamage: number;
  dead = false;
  protected attackTimer: number; // counts down to next attack
  abstract update(deltaMs, ctx: EnemyUpdateContext): void;
  applyDamage(instance): void; // handles chalk dust multipliers
  // view metadata
  abstract readonly textureKey: string | null;
  abstract readonly procedural: ProceduralShape | null;
}
interface EnemyUpdateContext {
  playerPosition: {x,y};
  timeMs: number;
  spawnProjectile(p): void;
  spawnZone(z): void;
  isWalkable(x,y): boolean;
  spawnEnemy?(factory): void; // for summon core
  rng?: ...;
}
```

This is a clean, testable interface. The CombatManager provides the context. Tests can construct an enemy, call update with a stub context that records spawned projectiles/zones, and assert.

Let me define DamageType.ts content:

```ts
export type DamageType = 'physical' | 'burn' | 'slow' | 'stun' | 'fear' | 'root';
export type DamageCategory = 'melee' | 'aoe' | 'dot';

export type DebuffType = 'burn' | 'slow' | 'stun' | 'fear' | 'root';

export interface BurnDebuff { type: 'burn'; dps: number; remainingMs: number; }
export interface SlowDebuff { type: 'slow'; multiplier: number; remainingMs: number; } // multiplier = fraction of speed retained (0.4 = 60% slow)
export interface StunDebuff { type: 'stun'; remainingMs: number; }
export interface FearDebuff { type: 'fear'; remainingMs: number; sourceX: number; sourceY: number; }
export interface RootDebuff { type: 'root'; remainingMs: number; }
export type Debuff = BurnDebuff | SlowDebuff | StunDebuff | FearDebuff | RootDebuff;

export interface DamageInstance { amount: number; type: DamageType; category: DamageCategory; }

// constants
export const PLAYER_MAX_HP = 100;
export const PLAYER_BASE_SPEED = 200;
export const PLAYER_CONTACT_DAMAGE_COOLDOWN_MS = 1000;
export const WEAK_PUNCH_DAMAGE = 5;
export const PLACEHOLDER_WEAPON_ID = 'weapon.ruler';
```

And a `DebuffTracker` class (the "state machine") that holds active debuffs, ticks them, and reports movement override. Actually the task says "伤害类型/debuff 枚举与状态机". So DamageType.ts contains the debuff state machine. I'll put a `DebuffTracker` class here that PlayerCombat uses. Let me include it.

```ts
export interface MovementOverride {
  readonly locked: boolean; // true if stun/root (no move) 
  readonly speedMultiplier: number; // 1 normal, 0 if locked, slowMult if slowed
  readonly fleeFrom: { x: number; y: number } | null; // fear
}

export class DebuffTracker {
  private burn: BurnDebuff | null = null;
  private slow: SlowDebuff | null = null;
  private stun: StunDebuff | null = null;
  private fear: FearDebuff | null = null;
  private root: RootDebuff | null = null;

  apply(debuff: Debuff): void { ... replace same-type, keep others ... }
  has(type): boolean
  get(type): Debuff | null
  clear(): void
  tick(deltaMs): { burnDamage: number } // returns burn damage to apply this tick
  getMovementOverride(): MovementOverride
  list(): Debuff[]
}
```

Note: slow multiplier — if multiple slows, take the strongest (lowest multiplier). Burn — refresh dps/duration. Fear source — for flee direction.

MovementOverride logic:
- if stun or root active → locked true, multiplier 0, fleeFrom null
- else if fear active → locked false, multiplier 1 (can move but forced flee), fleeFrom = fear source. Actually fear makes you flee — forced movement. Is that "locked"? No, you move (away). But the player isn't controlling. I'll model: fear → locked=false but the scene should apply flee movement; speedMultiplier=1. The scene checks fleeFrom. Hmm, but if also slowed... Let me keep: fear sets fleeFrom; if slow also active, speedMultiplier = slow multiplier. If stun/root, locked wins.
- else if slow → locked false, multiplier = slow.multiplier, fleeFrom null
- else normal: locked false, multiplier 1, fleeFrom null

Good.

export type DebuffType = 'burn' | 'slow' | 'stun' | 'fear' | 'root';

For CombatManager, the update loop:
```
update(deltaMs):
  clamp delta
  player.tick(deltaMs) // ticks debuffs, applies burn damage to HP, checks death
  for each enemy alive: enemy.update(deltaMs, ctx)
  move projectiles, homing, lifetime, collide with player → damage
  tick zones (windup→active→expire), apply zone damage to player if in radius, apply to enemies? (player attacks are melee/aoe handled separately)
  contact damage: for each enemy, if dist(enemy,player) < contactRadius and enemy.contactTimer<=0 → apply enemy.contactDamage melee to player, reset timer
  decrement timers
  remove dead/expired
  handle body revive scheduling, body cap, etc. (these live in enemy but need manager hooks)
```

Player attack:
```
playerAttack(direction):
  // placeholder weak punch: melee arc in front of player, 5 physical melee
  hitbox = arc/rect in front of playerPosition along direction, range ~60, half-angle 45
  for each enemy alive not dead: if enemy intersects hitbox → enemy.applyDamage({amount:5, category:'melee'})
  (real weapons plan 4)
```

Let me define Projectile:
```ts
export interface Projectile {
  id: string;
  x: number; y: number;
  vx: number; vy: number;
  speed: number;
  damage: number;
  category: DamageCategory;
  debuff?: Debuff;
  homingTarget: 'player' | null;
  homingStrength: number; // turn rate
  remainingMs: number;
  radius: number;
  proceduralKind: 'danYuxuanOrb' | 'bloodEyeOrb' | 'woodChip' | null;
  ownerId: string; // enemy id
}
```

ZoneEffect:
```ts
export interface ZoneEffect {
  id: string;
  x: number; y: number;
  radius: number;       // for circular zones
  width?: number;       // for line/wave zones (floor crack)
  height?: number;
  shape: 'circle' | 'line';
  remainingMs: number;
  windupMs: number;     // >0 = warning phase, no damage
  damagePerTick: number; // applied per second while active and player inside
  tickAccumulator: number;
  category: DamageCategory;
  debuff?: Debuff;
  proceduralKind: 'phoneRedCircle' | 'phoneExplosion' | 'phoneRinging' | 'screamWave' | 'floorCrack' | 'chalkCloud' | null;
  ownerId: string;
  vx?: number; vy?: number; // for moving waves (floor crack)
}
```
Hmm, floor crack is a moving wave (width 60, speed 200). Phone is windup circle then explosion then ringing residual. These are different. To avoid overcomplicating ZoneEffect, I'll let enemies manage their own multi-phase zones via multiple ZoneEffect instances or internal timers. Actually simpler: let enemies that need complex effects (phone, floor crack, scream) spawn zones with appropriate fields and let CombatManager apply zone damage generically (windup → active → expire; if player in radius and active → damage). For phone: spawn a windup circle (1.2s, no dmg), then on expire spawn explosion circle (instant 30 dmg burst) + ringing circle (2s, 10 dmg over 2s = 5/s? spec says "留振铃区 2s 内再伤 10" = 10 more over 2s). I'll model explosion as a zone with remainingMs small and a burst damage flag. This is getting complicated.

Let me simplify the zone model to handle the cases:
- Windup-only zone (no damage, just visual + on expire triggers something). But "on expire triggers" requires callbacks. Instead, the enemy itself tracks its attack phases via internal timers and spawns damage zones at the right time. E.g., phone enemy: attackTimer triggers; phase 1 (0-1.2s) spawn visual red circle zone (windupMs, no dmg); at 1.2s spawn explosion zone (instant burst 30) + ringing zone (2s DoT 5/s totaling 10). The enemy's update manages these phase transitions by checking elapsed since attack start.

To keep CombatManager generic, ZoneEffect supports:
- `windupMs`: during windup, no damage.
- `burstDamage`: if >0, applied once instantly when windup ends (then zone becomes a DoT or expires).
- `damagePerSecond`: applied while active (post-windup) and player inside.
- `debuff`: applied on hit.
- moving: `vx,vy` for waves.
- `shape: 'circle'|'rect'`, radius/width/height.

CombatManager.tick for a zone:
```
if windupMs>0: windupMs-=dt; if windupMs<=0: if burstDamage>0 and player in zone: apply burst; (then active phase)
else: remainingMs-=dt; damagePerSecond accumulates; if player in zone: apply dps*dt; apply debuff once on entry
move by vx,vy
if remainingMs<=0: remove
```

That handles phone (windup circle → on windup end apply burst 30 + spawn separate ringing DoT zone), floor crack (rect wave, moving, no windup or 0.6s windup red line then active moving wave dealing 28 + slow), scream wave (circle, 18 dmg + slow, maybe instant burst), chalk cloud (persistent circle, 5/s, vision debuff — but chalk cloud is the ENEMY itself, not a zone; its contact DoT is handled by enemy contact). 

Hmm, chalk dust is an enemy that itself is a zone-like cloud. Its "持续接触伤害 5/s" is contact damage (per second while player touching the cloud enemy). I'll handle chalk dust contact as a special contact-damage case (per-second, not per-cooldown). And vision reduction is an event emitted while player in range.

OK. Let me finalize ZoneEffect fields:
```ts
export interface ZoneEffect {
  id: string;
  shape: 'circle' | 'rect';
  x: number; y: number;
  radius: number;     // circle
  width: number;      // rect
  height: number;     // rect
  angle: number;      // rect rotation (radians) for directional waves
  vx: number; vy: number; // movement per second
  windupMs: number;   // warning phase, no damage
  burstDamage: number; // applied once when windup ends (if player in zone)
  damagePerSecond: number; // DoT while active
  category: DamageCategory;
  debuff?: Debuff;
  remainingMs: number; // total lifetime (incl windup)
  applyDebuffOnce: boolean;
  debuffApplied: boolean;
  proceduralKind: string | null;
  ownerId: string;
}
```

CombatManager will apply: during windup no dmg; when windup ends and burstDamage>0 → check player in zone → apply burst + debuff; after windup → dps ticking + debuff on entry. For moving rect (floor crack), "player in zone" = point-in-rotated-rect. I'll implement a `pointInZone` helper.

Enemy base:
```ts
export abstract class Enemy {
  abstract readonly kind: EnemyKind;
  readonly id: string;
  x: number; y: number;
  hp: number;
  readonly maxHp: number;
  readonly speed: number;
  readonly contactDamage: number;
  readonly contactRadius: number;
  dead = false;
  contactCooldownMs = 0;
  parentId: string | null = null; // for bound heads
  abstract readonly textureKey: string | null;
  abstract readonly proceduralKind: ProceduralKind | null;
  tint: { color: number; alpha: number } | null = null; // for phantoms
  overlay: 'bloodEye' | null = null;

  constructor(opts: { id; x; y; maxHp; speed; contactDamage; contactRadius }) {...}
  applyDamage(instance: DamageInstance, _source?: ...): void {
    let amount = instance.amount;
    if (this.kind === 'chalkDust') {
      if (instance.category === 'melee') amount *= 0.5;
      else if (instance.category === 'aoe') amount *= 1.5;
    }
    if (amount <= 0) return;
    this.hp = Math.max(0, this.hp - amount);
    if (this.hp <= 0) this.dead = true;
  }
  distanceTo(x, y): number
  abstract update(deltaMs, ctx): void;
}
```

Hmm chalk dust multiplier hard-coded in base applyDamage via kind check — acceptable. Or override applyDamage in chalk dust. I'll override in chalk dust subclass for cleanliness and keep base generic. Actually base applyDamage generic; chalk dust overrides. Good.

Actually, in TypeScript, you CAN override a base class property (field) with a getter in a subclass. So:

- Subclass YangYunRedEnemy: `get contactBurn(): ContactBurn | null { return this.contactBurnValue; }`

13. DanYuxuanBodyEnemy — 召唤核心（机制 A：30s 召唤血瞳/上限 3）
14. YangYunRedEnemy — 精英怪（机制 A–E：冲撞/幻影/地板裂波/二阶段暴怒/死亡掉落）
15. EnemyViewRenderer — 集中式程序绘制渲染器（贴图/程序绘制/血瞳叠加/幻影 tint）
16. CombatManager 集成烟雾测试 — 11 种 factory 全注册 + 端到端

Let me design it this way:
- `WeaponCombatAdapter` is the plan 4 module that owns weapon attack resolution.
- It takes a `CombatPort` interface (subset of CombatManager: `playerX/Y`, `enemies`, `damageEnemy`, `spawnProjectile`, `spawnZone`, `killEnemyImmediate`) + `player: PlayerCombat` + `weaponEffect: WeaponEffect`.
- `adapter.performAttack(direction)`: reads current weapon, resolves hits per attack type, applies damage via combatPort, fires visuals via weaponEffect, enforces attack speed CD.
- `adapter.performUltimate(direction)`: reads current weapon ultimate, resolves effect, applies damage/AoE/instant-kill, fires visuals, enforces CD.

Now for fear flee-override and stun/root skip in the enemy loop: I'll add to CombatManager.update()'s enemy loop. But I said I'd avoid editing plan 3's update loop heavily. Let me reconsider: the enemy loop in CombatManager.update() (lines 1343-1347) is:
```
for (const enemy of this.enemies) {
  if (enemy.dead) continue;
  if (enemy.contactCooldownMs > 0) enemy.contactCooldownMs -= dt;
  enemy.update(dt, ctx);
}
```
Plan 4 needs to insert status handling. Editing this loop is editing plan 3's CombatManager. It's tombraid code (allowed). To minimize risk, I'll restructure minimally:
```
for (const enemy of this.enemies) {
  if (enemy.dead) continue;
  if (enemy.contactCooldownMs > 0) enemy.contactCooldownMs -= dt;
  enemy.tickStatus(dt); // additive: burn DoT to enemy hp, decrement stun/root/fear
  if (enemy.dead) continue; // burn may have killed
  if (enemy.isStunned() || enemy.isRooted()) {
    continue; // frozen: skip AI/movement
  }
  if (enemy.getFleeFrom() !== null) {
    this.moveEnemyFleeing(enemy, dt); // override movement
    continue;
  }
  enemy.update(dt, ctx);
}
```
This is a clean, contained insertion. Risk: changes enemy-loop behavior only when status is active (plan 3 enemies never have status → no behavior change → no regression).

OK. Let me also define the WeaponDef structure precisely:

```ts
export type WeaponRarity = 'purple' | 'green' | 'gold' | 'white';

export type WeaponBasicAttackKind =
  | 'meleeFan'      // 断尺/尺子/拳套/锁链/血镰/万魂幡
  | 'rangedPiercing' // 灵刃月牙剑气(穿透), 粉笔投掷(穿透1)
  | 'multiHitMelee'; // 拳套 10×3 (actually 拳套 is 快速连击10×3 — multi-hit)

export type WeaponUltimateKind =
  | 'scatterShards'    // 断尺尺屑散射 6发4伤
  | 'chalkBombAoe'     // 粉笔爆弹 AoE 25
  | 'rulerStorm'       // 尺子风暴 旋转AoE 2s
  | 'bladeArray'       // 万刃阵 8方向齐射
  | 'fistDash'         // 霸体冲拳 无敌突进 80总伤
  | 'chainCrush'       // 万锁绞杀 群拉+缚身2s+持续伤
  | 'bloodWheel'       // 血轮 周身旋转3s高伤
  | 'soulCapture';     // 拘魂 秒杀

export interface WeaponBasicAttack {
  readonly damage: number;
  readonly attacksPerSecond: number;
  readonly kind: WeaponBasicAttackKind;
  readonly range: number;          // melee fan range / ranged projectile travel
  readonly halfAngle: number;      // radians, melee fan half-angle (0 for ranged)
  readonly category: DamageCategory;
  readonly pierceCount?: number;   // ranged piercing (粉笔=1, 灵刃=Infinity)
  readonly hitsPerAttack?: number; // 拳套 multi-hit (10)
  readonly hitDelayMs?: number;    // 拳套 multi-hit delay
  readonly lifestealPercent?: number; // 血镰 10
  readonly fearProcPercent?: number;  // 万魂幡 20
  readonly fearDurationMs?: number;   // 万魂幡 2000
  readonly projectileSpeed?: number;  // ranged
  readonly projectileRadius?: number;
  readonly effectKind: WeaponEffectKind; // for rendering
}

export interface WeaponUltimate {
  readonly kind: WeaponUltimateKind;
  readonly cooldownMs: number;
  readonly damage?: number;          // per-hit or burst
  readonly durationMs?: number;      // sustained (rulerStorm 2000, bloodWheel 3000)
  readonly radius?: number;
  readonly shardCount?: number;      // 断尺 6
  readonly totalDamage?: number;     // 拳套 80
  readonly invincibleMs?: number;    // 拳套 dash invincibility
  readonly rootMs?: number;          // 锁链 2000
  readonly pullRadius?: number;      // 锁链
  readonly captureRadius?: number;   // 万魂幡
  readonly excludeKinds?: readonly EnemyKind[]; // 万魂幡 excludes yangYunRed
  readonly effectKind: WeaponEffectKind;
}

export interface WeaponDef {
  readonly id: WeaponId;
  readonly name: string;
  readonly rarity: WeaponRarity;
  readonly sanityValue: number;
  readonly textureKey: string | null; // null = procedural; 'prop.ruler' for 尺子
  readonly basic: WeaponBasicAttack;
  readonly ultimate: WeaponUltimate;
  readonly description: string;
}
```

Let me simplify the whole rendering contract:
- `WeaponEffect.ts` exports:
  - `drawWeaponProjectile(graphics, kind: WeaponProjectileKind, x, y, angle, radius)` — draws a player projectile of given kind.
  - `drawWeaponZone(graphics, kind: WeaponZoneKind, x, y, radius, alpha)` — draws a player zone.
  - `drawMeleeFlash(graphics, kind: MeleeFlashKind, x, y, dirX, dirY, range, halfAngle, progress)` — draws the transient melee slash arc.
- Where `WeaponProjectileKind`, `WeaponZoneKind`, `MeleeFlashKind` are string unions defined in WeaponEffect.ts.
- ProceduralKind (Enemy.ts) extended with the projectile+zone kinds so CombatManager projectiles/zones can carry them.

But Projectile.proceduralKind is `ProceduralKind` (a single union for both enemy and player projectiles). So the player projectile kinds must be in ProceduralKind. Similarly ZoneEffect.proceduralKind: ProceduralKind | null — player zone kinds in ProceduralKind.

So I'll extend ProceduralKind to add the 9 weapon effect kinds (rulerShard, chalkThrow, chalkBomb, rulerStorm, bladeCrescent, fistDash, chainCrush, bloodWheel, soulCapture). The melee-flash kinds (brokenRulerSlash etc.) are NOT in ProceduralKind (they're not projectiles/zones) — they're a separate union in WeaponEffect.ts.

```ts
export interface MeleeFanBasic {
  readonly kind: 'meleeFan';
  readonly damage: number;
  readonly attacksPerSecond: number;
  readonly range: number;
  readonly halfAngle: number; // radians
  readonly hitsPerAttack: number;
  readonly category: DamageCategory;
  readonly lifestealPercent: number;       // 0 default
  readonly fearProcPercent: number;        // 0 default
  readonly fearDurationMs: number;         // 0 default
  readonly effectKind: MeleeFlashKind;
}
export interface RangedPiercingBasic {
  readonly kind: 'rangedPiercing';
  readonly damage: number;
  readonly attacksPerSecond: number;
  readonly range: number;                  // projectile travel distance
  readonly pierceCount: number;            // Infinity for infinite
  readonly projectileSpeed: number;
  readonly projectileRadius: number;
  readonly category: DamageCategory;
  readonly effectKind: WeaponProjectileKind;
}
export type WeaponBasicAttack = MeleeFanBasic | RangedPiercingBasic;
```

Discriminated union — clean, strict-safe. For 拳套 (meleeFan, hitsPerAttack 10). For 灵刃/粉笔 (rangedPiercing). For 断尺/尺子/锁链/血镰/万魂幡 (meleeFan hitsPerAttack 1).

export interface PlayerZone {
  id: string;
  shape: 'circle';
  x: number; y: number;
  radius: number;
  burstDamage: number;        // applied once at spawn to enemies inside
  damagePerSecond: number;    // DoT while active

  debuffApplied: boolean;

}

pullEnemiesToward(cx, cy, radius, pullDistance): for each enemy in radius, move it toward (cx,cy) by pullDistance (capped to not overshoot). For 锁链万锁.

damageEnemiesInFan(originX, originY, dirX, dirY, range, halfAngle, instance): returns total damage dealt. Applies damage + debuff.

OK. Now let me also handle the enemy-loop status gating edit. The plan-3 loop:
```ts
for (const enemy of this.enemies) {
  if (enemy.dead) continue;
  if (enemy.contactCooldownMs > 0) enemy.contactCooldownMs -= dt;
  enemy.update(dt, ctx);
}

New:
```ts
for (const enemy of this.enemies) {
  if (enemy.dead) continue;
  if (enemy.contactCooldownMs > 0) enemy.contactCooldownMs -= dt;
  enemy.tickStatus(dt);
  if (enemy.dead) continue;
  if (enemy.isStunned() || enemy.isRooted()) {
    continue; // frozen by weapon CC
  }
  const fleeFrom = enemy.getFleeFrom();
  if (fleeFrom !== null) {
    this.moveEnemyFleeing(enemy, dt, fleeFrom);
    continue;
  }
  enemy.update(dt, ctx);
}
```
moveEnemyFleeing: move enemy away from fleeFrom at enemy.speed.

I need `tickStatus`, `isStunned`, `isRooted`, `getFleeFrom` on Enemy (Task 1). Good.

Now, does editing this loop risk breaking plan-3 tests? Plan-3 enemies never have status (tickStatus no-ops, isStunned false, getFleeFrom null) → behavior identical. Zero regression.

Good. Now WeaponCooldowns gating: executeBasicAttack checks `cooldowns.canBasicAttack(timeMs)` (based on attacksPerSecond → cooldownMs = 1000/aps); if not, return. Else record. executeUltimate checks `cooldowns.canUltimate(timeMs)`; if not, return false. Else record (cooldownMs from weapon.ultimate.cooldownMs). 

WeaponCooldowns design:
```ts
export class WeaponCooldowns {
  private basicReadyAtMs = 0;
  private ultimateReadyAtMs = 0;
  canBasicAttack(timeMs: number): boolean { return timeMs >= this.basicReadyAtMs; }
  recordBasicAttack(weapon: WeaponDef, timeMs: number): void {
    const cdMs = 1000 / weapon.basic.attacksPerSecond;
    this.basicReadyAtMs = timeMs + cdMs;
  }
  canUltimate(timeMs: number): boolean { return timeMs >= this.ultimateReadyAtMs; }
  recordUltimate(weapon: WeaponDef, timeMs: number): void {
    this.ultimateReadyAtMs = timeMs + weapon.ultimate.cooldownMs;
  }
  onWeaponSwap(): void { this.basicReadyAtMs = 0; this.ultimateReadyAtMs = 0; } // swap resets CD

}
```

  g.clear();
  g.fillStyle(UI_THEME.colors.gold, 0.9);
  g.fillCircle(x, y, radius);

I need to fix `applyDamageInstanceToEnemy` so that amount<=0 still applies the debuff. Let me restructure:
```
private applyDamageInstanceToEnemy(enemy, instance): number {
  if (enemy.dead) return 0;
  let dealt = 0;
  if (instance.amount > 0) {
    const before = enemy.hp;
    enemy.applyDamage(instance);
    dealt = before - enemy.hp;
  }
  if (instance.debuff) enemy.applyDebuff(instance.debuff);
  return dealt;
}
```
This applies debuff regardless of amount. Good. Let me fix Task 2's `applyDamageInstanceToEnemy`.

But wait — does this break the Task 2 "applies debuff to hit enemies (万魂幡 fear)" test? That test passes amount 5 + fear debuff via damageEnemiesInFan. enemy in fan, amount 5 > 0 → applyDamage (5 dmg) + applyDebuff(fear). getFleeFrom = {0,0}. Good. And the fan filter: `inFan` true. Good.

- Task 2 playerProjectile pierce test: e1(50,0,10), e2(80,0,10), e3(110,0,10). projectile vx=300, from (0,0). update(400): travels to x=120. Hit-test loop: for each enemy in enemies list order [e1,e2,e3], check dist. At update time, projectile has moved to x = 0 + 300*0.4 = 120. So after moving, p.x=120. Then hit-test: e1 at 50 → dist |120-50|=70 > radius 12 + contactRadius 24 = 36? 70 > 36 → no hit. Hmm! That's wrong.

The issue: my updatePlayerProjectiles moves the projectile the FULL dt first, THEN hit-tests. So in one update(400), projectile jumps from 0 to 120, skipping over e1(50) and e2(80). Only e3(110) is within 36 of 120? |120-110|=10 ≤ 36 → hit e3. But the test expects e1 and e2 hit, e3 not. So my single-step movement breaks pierce.

This is a real bug. Plan 3's enemy projectiles have the same issue but they're slower / collision is point-based. For player projectiles with pierce, I need sub-stepping or hit-test along the path.

Options:
1. Sub-step the projectile movement (e.g. move in small increments, hit-test each).
2. Hit-test against the segment swept this frame (segment-circle intersection).

For correctness and simplicity, sub-stepping is easiest. Let me sub-step: divide dt into steps such that each step moves ≤ radius (so no tunneling). 

Actually, simpler robust approach: for each projectile, move in steps of `max(radius, enemy.contactRadius)` distance, hit-testing at each step. Let me implement sub-stepping based on speed.

Let me rewrite updatePlayerProjectiles with sub-stepping:
```
private updatePlayerProjectiles(dt: number): void {
  const maxStep = 8; // px per sub-step (small to avoid tunneling)
  for (const p of this.playerProjectiles) {
    const totalDist = p.speed * (dt / 1000);
    const steps = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(totalDist / maxStep));
    const stepDist = totalDist / steps;
    const stepDt = dt / steps;
    let traveled = 0;
    for (let s = 0; s < steps; s++) {
      const nx = p.vx / p.speed, ny = p.vy / p.speed;
      p.x += nx * stepDist; p.y += ny * stepDist;
      traveled += stepDist;
      p.remainingMs -= stepDt;
      for (const enemy of this.enemies) {
        if (enemy.dead) continue;
        if (p.pierceRemaining < 0) break;
        const dist = Math.hypot(enemy.x - p.x, enemy.y - p.y);
        if (dist <= p.radius + enemy.contactRadius) {
          this.applyDamageInstanceToEnemy(enemy, {amount:p.damage, category:p.category, ...(p.debuff?{debuff:p.debuff}:{})});
          if (p.pierceRemaining === Infinity) continue;
          p.pierceRemaining -= 1;
        }
      }
      if (p.pierceRemaining < 0 || p.remainingMs <= 0) break;
    }
  }
  // prune
  for (let i = this.playerProjectiles.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    const p = this.playerProjectiles[i]!;
    if (p.remainingMs <= 0 || p.pierceRemaining < 0) this.playerProjectiles.splice(i, 1);
  }
}
```
With maxStep 8 and speed 300, dt 400 → totalDist 120, steps = ceil(120/8)=15, stepDist 8. Projectile moves 8px per sub-step, hit-testing each. At x~50 it hits e1, at x~80 hits e2 (pierceRemaining 1→0 after e1, then... wait pierceRemaining starts 1. Hit e1: pierce 1→0. Hit e2: pierce 0→-1. Now `p.pierceRemaining < 0` → break inner, and outer `if (p.pierceRemaining < 0) break;`. So projectile stops at e2. e3 not hit. 

But the test expects e1.hp=0, e2.hp=0, e3.hp=10, and playerProjectiles length 0. With pierceRemaining=1 (meaning "pierce 1 => hits 2 total" per test comment): starts at 1. Hit e1 → decrement to 0. Hit e2 → decrement to -1 → break. So 2 hits total (e1, e2). e3 untouched. Then prune: pierceRemaining < 0 → removed. length 0. 

But wait — `p.vx / p.speed` = 300/300 = 1 (unit x). Good. p.vy/speed = 0. So moves along +x. Good.

Hmm, but the sub-step hit-test: when projectile is at x≈48 (step), dist to e1(50) = 2 ≤ 12+24=36 → hit e1. Good. Continues to x≈56,80,... at x≈80 hits e2. Good.

Edge: after hitting e1 (pierce 1→0), the inner `for enemy` loop continues to e2 in the SAME sub-step position? At x≈48 sub-step, only e1 is within 36 (e2 at 80, dist 32 ≤ 36 — also within!). So in the same sub-step at x=48, e1 (dist 2) AND e2 (dist 32) both within 36 → both hit in same sub-step. pierce: e1 1→0, e2 0→-1 → break. So both hit at x≈48. e3(110) dist 62 > 36 → not hit. Good. Result: e1, e2 dead, e3 alive, projectile removed. Test passes.

Actually, let me reconsider the whole pierce semantics. `pierceRemaining`: 
- 0 = hits 1 enemy then removed.
- 1 = hits 2 enemies then removed.
- Infinity = hits unlimited, removed only by time/range.

My impl: on hit, if `pierceRemaining === Infinity` continue (don't decrement); else `pierceRemaining -= 1`. Prune if `pierceRemaining < 0`. So:
- pierceRemaining 0: hit → 0→-1 → prune. (1 hit) ✓
- pierceRemaining 1: hit → 1→0 (alive, 1 hit), next hit → 0→-1 → prune (2 hits). ✓
- Infinity: never decrements, never < 0, pruned only by remainingMs. ✓

Good. Let me fix test 1 to pierceRemaining 0, and add an Infinity test.

```
describe('spawnPlayerProjectile — damages enemies, not player', () => {
  it('hit-once projectile (pierceRemaining 0) damages enemy and is removed; player hp unchanged', () => {
    const m = makeManager();
    m.setPlayerPosition(0, 0);
    const enemy = new DummyEnemy(200, 0, 45);
    m.addEnemy(enemy);
    m.spawnPlayerProjectile({ id:'pp1', x:0,y:0, vx:200,vy:0, speed:200, damage:18, category:'melee', pierceRemaining:0, remainingMs:2000, radius:12, proceduralKind:'bladeCrescent' });
    m.update(1000);

    expect(m.player.hp).toBe(100);
    expect(m.playerProjectiles).toHaveLength(0);
  });

  it('finite pierce (pierceRemaining 1) hits 2 enemies then is removed', () => {
    ... e1(50,0,10), e2(80,0,10), e3(110,0,10); pierceRemaining 1; update(400)
    expect e1,e2 dead; e3 alive; length 0
  });

  it('infinite pierce (Infinity) hits all enemies along path and survives until expiry', () => {

    m.addEnemy(e1); m.addEnemy(e2);

    m.update(500); // travels to x=150, hits both
    expect(e1.hp).toBe(0); expect(e2.hp).toBe(0);
    expect(m.playerProjectiles).toHaveLength(1); // still alive (infinite pierce, remainingMs 1500)
  });
});

```ts
export interface WeaponExecutionContext {
  readonly manager: CombatManager;
  readonly cooldowns: WeaponCooldowns;
  timeMs: number;          // mutable: scene advances this each frame
  readonly rng: () => number;
}
```

Now, `import type { RootDebuff }` in Task 8 — appended at top? No, I put it at the start of Task 8's appended block: `import type { RootDebuff } from '../combat/DamageType';`. But ES module imports must be at the TOP of the file, not mid-file! Task 7 also appends `import { WEAK_PUNCH_DAMAGE, type DamageInstance, type FearDebuff } from '../combat/DamageType';` mid-file. And Task 6 appends `import type { CombatManager, ... }` mid-file.

This is the SAME issue plan 3 flagged (the note about imports must be at top). TypeScript/esbuild will error: "import declarations must be at the top level of a module" — actually, ES module spec allows imports only at top level (not inside blocks), but they can appear anywhere at the top level of the module (interspersed with other statements). Actually, ES module `import` statements are hoisted and must be at the top level (not nested), but they CAN appear after other top-level statements? The spec says import declarations must be at module top level. They are hoisted. Most bundlers allow them interspersed at top level. But `@typescript-eslint` no; tsc allows top-level imports anywhere at top level (they're hoisted). Actually tsc DOES allow imports not at the very top, as long as they're top-level. E.g.:
```ts
export const x = 1;
import { y } from './y';
```
This is valid TS (imports are hoisted). esbuild handles it. So appending import statements after exports is fine syntactically. But it's bad style. The plan 3 note addressed exactly this. 

To be safe and clean, I should consolidate: have Task 6/7/8 note that their imports should be merged into the file's top import block. But for a plan, the cleanest is to put ALL imports at the top in Task 3 (the file creation), anticipating Task 6/7/8 needs. But Task 3 doesn't know future imports... For plan 5, the EFFECTS are defined as data (the actual effect application is plan 3 CombatManager / future tasks). So LootItem just carries an `effect` description object. I'll define a discriminated union LootEffect.

Let me define:
```ts
export type LootEffect =
  | { kind: 'heal'; amount: number; castTimeMs: number }
  | { kind: 'cleanse'; castTimeMs: number }
  | { kind: 'buff'; stat: 'moveSpeed' | 'attackSpeed' | 'visionRange' | 'pickupRange' | 'critRate' | 'basicDamage'; magnitudePercent: number; durationMs: number }
  | { kind: 'invulnerable'; durationMs: number; castTimeMs: number; fullRestore: boolean }
  | { kind: 'aoeCrowdControl'; ccType: 'stun'; durationMs: number; vulnerabilityBonusPercent: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveMaxHp'; amount: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveStat'; stat: 'moveSpeed' | 'attackSpeed' | 'visionRange' | 'pickupRange' | 'critRate' | 'basicDamage' | 'pickupRadius'; magnitudePercent: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveConsumableStackBonus'; amount: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveDamageImmunityChance'; chancePercent: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveBasicDamagePercent'; magnitudePercent: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveAttackSpeedWithHpPenalty'; attackSpeedPercent: number; maxHpDelta: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveExtractionValueBonus'; magnitudePercent: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveReviveOnce'; reviveHpPercent: number };
```

Let me think about Task 4 (ChestDecrypt). This is a Phaser animation state machine. Since plan 2's TombRaidMapRenderer test uses mock Phaser (per summary "mock phaser"), I'll do the same for ChestDecrypt. The state machine is:
- States: 'idle' | 'decrypting' | 'opened' | 'completed'
- Progress: 0..1, advances while F held, pauses on release (no regression)
- 4 lock milestones at 0.25/0.5/0.75/1.0
- Total duration ~2.5s → progress rate = 1/2500 per ms
- On complete: spawn loot cards

The state machine logic (progress, milestones, hold/pause) is testable WITHOUT Phaser. The Phaser rendering (drawing code rings, particles, etc.) is the rendering part. Following plan 2's pattern, I'll split:
- `ChestDecryptState.ts` (pure state machine, no Phaser) — testable
- `ChestDecrypt.ts` (Phaser rendering, thin) — uses the state

LootEffect variants needed:
Consumables:
- 薄荷糖: heal 3, cast 0 → { kind:'heal'; amount:3; castTimeMs:0 }
- 过期眼药水: buff visionRange +10% 10s → { kind:'buff'; stat:'visionRange'; magnitudePercent:10; durationMs:10000 }
- 半瓶矿泉水: buff moveSpeed +5% 8s → { kind:'buff'; stat:'moveSpeed'; magnitudePercent:5; durationMs:8000 }
- 芹菜: heal 30, cast 500 → { kind:'heal'; amount:30; castTimeMs:500 }
- 解药: cleanse, cast 300 → { kind:'cleanse'; castTimeMs:300 }
- 肾上腺素: buff moveSpeed+30% & attackSpeed+20% 8s → TWO buffs. Hmm, single buff variant only does one stat. Need multi-buff. → { kind:'multiBuff'; buffs:[{stat:'moveSpeed',magnitudePercent:30},{stat:'attackSpeed',magnitudePercent:20}]; durationMs:8000 }
- 圣水: invulnerable 3s + fullRestore, cast 1000 → { kind:'invulnerable'; durationMs:3000; castTimeMs:1000; fullRestore:true }
- 镇魂铃: aoeCC stun 5s + vuln +30% → { kind:'aoeStun'; durationMs:5000; vulnerabilityBonusPercent:30 }

Relics (passives):
- 褪色学生卡: maxHp +5 → { kind:'passiveMaxHp'; amount:5 }
- 磨旧橡皮: pickupRange +10% → { kind:'passiveStat'; stat:'pickupRange'; magnitudePercent:10 }
- 破洞书包: consumable stack +5 → { kind:'passiveConsumableStackBonus'; amount:5 }
- 蓝边发带: maxHp +20 → { kind:'passiveMaxHp'; amount:20 }
- 但宇轩眼镜: visionRange +20% → { kind:'passiveStat'; stat:'visionRange'; magnitudePercent:20 }
- 秦浩睿尺规: critRate +8% → { kind:'passiveStat'; stat:'critRate'; magnitudePercent:8 }
- 血渍绷带: 15% chance no damage → { kind:'passiveDamageImmunityChance'; chancePercent:15 }
- 拳击手套: basicDamage +20% → { kind:'passiveStat'; stat:'basicDamage'; magnitudePercent:20 }
- 红边发带: attackSpeed +25%, maxHp -15 → { kind:'passiveStatWithHpPenalty'; stat:'attackSpeed'; magnitudePercent:25; maxHpDelta:-15 }
- 校长印章: extraction value +15% → { kind:'passiveExtractionValueBonus'; magnitudePercent:15 }
- 黑色毕业照: revive once, 50% hp → { kind:'passiveReviveOnce'; reviveHpPercent:50 }

Materials & treasures: effect = null.

So LootEffect union:
```ts
export type LootEffect =
  | { kind: 'heal'; amount: number; castTimeMs: number }
  | { kind: 'cleanse'; castTimeMs: number }
  | { kind: 'buff'; stat: LootBuffStat; magnitudePercent: number; durationMs: number }
  | { kind: 'multiBuff'; buffs: ReadonlyArray<{ stat: LootBuffStat; magnitudePercent: number }>; durationMs: number }
  | { kind: 'invulnerable'; durationMs: number; castTimeMs: number; fullRestore: boolean }
  | { kind: 'aoeStun'; durationMs: number; vulnerabilityBonusPercent: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveMaxHp'; amount: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveStat'; stat: LootPassiveStat; magnitudePercent: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveStatWithHpPenalty'; stat: 'attackSpeed'; magnitudePercent: number; maxHpDelta: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveConsumableStackBonus'; amount: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveDamageImmunityChance'; chancePercent: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveExtractionValueBonus'; magnitudePercent: number }
  | { kind: 'passiveReviveOnce'; reviveHpPercent: number };

export type LootBuffStat = 'moveSpeed' | 'attackSpeed' | 'visionRange' | 'pickupRange';
export type LootPassiveStat = 'moveSpeed' | 'attackSpeed' | 'visionRange' | 'pickupRange' | 'critRate' | 'basicDamage';
```

LootItem interface:
```ts
export type LootRarity = 'blue' | 'purple' | 'green' | 'gold' | 'white';
export type LootType = 'material' | 'consumable' | 'relic' | 'weapon' | 'treasure';

export interface LootItem {
  readonly id: string;
  readonly name: string;
  readonly rarity: LootRarity;
  readonly type: LootType;
  readonly sanityValue: number;
  readonly spriteKey: string;  // asset key for inventory/loot-card texture
  readonly description: string;
  readonly effect: LootEffect | null;
}
```
